saida_bruta:
“You look familiar,” he remarked politely. “Weren’t you with the First Division during the war?”

“Yes, I served in the Twenty-eighth Infantry.”

“I was in the Sixteenth until June of ’18. That’s why you seemed familiar.”

We briefly reminisced about dreary, rain-soaked villages in France. It turned out he lived nearby, and mentioned he’d just purchased a hydroplane and planned to test it out the next morning.

“Care to join me, old sport? We’ll just skim along the Sound by the shore.”

“What time should we go?”

“Whenever works for you.”

I was about to ask his name when Jordan glanced over and smiled.

“Are you enjoying yourself?” she asked.

“Much more now.” I turned back to my companion. “This sort of gathering isn’t really my scene—I haven’t even met the host. I actually live just over there—” I gestured toward the unseen hedge, “and Gatsby sent his driver with an invitation.”

He studied me for a moment as if puzzled.

“I’m Gatsby,” he said abruptly.

“Oh! I’m sorry,” I blurted out.

“I assumed you knew, old sport. I’m not much of a host, I’m afraid.”

saida_bruta:
“You seem familiar,” he said with courtesy. “Were you in the First Division during the war?”

“I was with the Twenty-eighth Infantry.”

“I served in the Sixteenth until June ’18. That must be why you’re familiar to me.”

We exchanged memories of damp, gloomy French villages. He revealed he lived close by and had just bought a hydroplane, hoping to try it out the next morning.

“Would you like to come along? We’ll just glide over the water by the coast.”

“What time should we meet?”

“Anytime that suits you.”

I was about to inquire his name when Jordan caught my eye and smiled.

“Are you having a good time?” she asked.

“Much better now.” I faced my companion again. “I’m not really used to these parties—I haven’t even met the host. I actually live just over there—” I nodded toward the hedge, “and Gatsby’s driver brought me an invitation.”

He studied me, looking slightly perplexed.

“I’m Gatsby,” he said suddenly.

“Oh! Sorry,” I stammered.

“I thought you knew, old sport. I’m not much of a host, really.

“I was with the Twenty-eighth Infantry.”

“I served with the Sixteenth until June ’18. Maybe that’s why you seem familiar.”

We reminisced about dreary, rain-drenched French villages. He mentioned he lived nearby and had recently purchased a hydroplane, planning to try it out the next morning.

“Would you care to join me? We’ll just skim along the coastline.”

“What time works for you?”

“Anytime is fine.”

I was about to inquire about his name when Jordan glanced over and smiled.

“Are you enjoying yourself?” she asked.

“Much more now.” I turned to my new acquaintance. “I’m not accustomed to gatherings like this—I haven’t even met the host. I live just over there—” I gestured toward the hedge, “and Gatsby’s chauffeur brought my invitation.”

He seemed briefly confused.

“I’m Gatsby,” he said abruptly.

“Oh! My apologies,” I replied, startled.

“I assumed you knew, old sport. I’m not much of a host, honestly.

“Would you like to come along? We’ll just follow the shoreline.”

“You seem familiar,” he said, offering a friendly, open smile. “Did you serve in the First Division during the war?”

“I was with the Twenty-eighth Infantry.”

“I was with the Sixteenth until June ’18. That might explain it.”

We swapped stories about foggy, somber villages across France. He mentioned living nearby and having just bought a hydroplane, planning to fly it along the coast the next morning.

“Would you care to join me? It’ll be a short flight by the shore.”

“What time should I arrive?”

“Anytime you wish.”

Before I could ask his name, Jordan gave me a pointed glance.

“Are you enjoying yourself?” she asked.

“I am now,” I answered, looking at my new acquaintance. “I don’t usually attend these events—I haven’t even met the host. I actually live just over there—” I gestured toward the hedge, “and Gatsby’s chauffeur brought my invitation.”

He looked momentarily confused.

“I’m Gatsby,” he said abruptly.

“Oh! I apologize,” I said, taken aback.

“I assumed you knew, old sport. I’m not much of a host.

Before I could inquire about his name, Jordan shot me a meaningful look.

“Having a good time?” she asked.

“I am now,” I replied, glancing at the man beside me. “I rarely come to these gatherings—I haven’t even met the host. I live just over there—” I nodded toward the hedge—“and Gatsby’s driver delivered my invitation.”

He appeared briefly bewildered.

“I’m Gatsby,” he said suddenly.

“Oh! My apologies,” I stammered, surprised.

“I thought you knew, old sport. I’m not much of a host, really.

saida_bruta:
“You seem familiar,” he said, offering a friendly, welcoming smile. “Did you serve in the First Division during the war?”

“I was with the Twenty-eighth Infantry.”

“I was with the Sixteenth until June ’18. That might explain it.”

We swapped stories about somber, foggy villages across France. He mentioned he lived nearby and had just bought a hydroplane, planning to fly it up the coast the next morning.

“Care to join me? It’ll be a swift flight over the sea.”

“When should I arrive?”

“Anytime you wish.”

Before I could ask his name, Jordan gave me a pointed glance.

“Are you enjoying yourself?” she asked.

“I am now,” I answered, glancing at the man next to me. “I hardly ever attend these events—I haven’t even met the host. I live just beyond that hedge,” nodding in its direction, “and Gatsby’s driver brought me my invitation.”

He looked momentarily confused.

“I’m Gatsby,” he declared abruptly.

“Oh! Sorry,” I blurted, taken aback.

“I assumed you knew, old sport. I’m not much of a host, honestly.
